narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 57.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales just over the threshold for denial. Your profile stood out with a second-decile ranking in Year 1 grades—much lower than the typical student's sixth-decile standing—alongside a third-decile finish in Year 3, compared to peers averaging around the fifth or sixth. Your LSAT score of 35 fell short of the average 36.7, and your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 was below the dataset's 3.21 norm, while your upper-middle family income, male gender, white racial background, and full-time study plans aligned closely with the majority of students. When the model weighed these details, your Year 1 decile emerged as the single most powerful influence, nudging the prediction toward failure due to its signal of early academic struggles. Right behind it in impact was your Year 3 decile, reinforcing concerns about sustained performance relative to classmates. Your LSAT score ranked third in sway, as its below-average mark echoed patterns in students who later faltered on the bar. Family income came next, followed closely by undergraduate GPA, with your racial background, gender, and full-time status exerting the slightest pulls—all subtly elevating the risk because they matched profiles where passing rates dipped. Overall, your academic standings painted a picture riskier than the typical applicant who passes, whose metrics cluster higher across the board. The good news is several factors remain within your control to strengthen for a future application. Retaking the LSAT to push toward or above 37 could significantly shift the model's view, as it's a key influencer you can target directly. Similarly, building evidence of improved academic habits—through postgraduate courses or detailed explanations of past challenges—might offset those lower deciles in a holistic review. Focusing on these steps could realistically drop your predicted failure risk below 50%, opening doors to approval next time. Keep pushing; many students turn similar profiles around with targeted effort.
